setting.note: Marie-Sophie Laborieux tells an urban planner sent to raze her shantytown the whole 150-year history of her family and of Texaco, the improvised quarter she founded on company land above Fort-de-France, from slavery through emancipation to the modern city. Chamoiseau maps the real Martinique: the tin-and-timber quarter of Texaco clinging to the hillside, the city of Fort-de-France below, the plantations of the enslaved past, and old Saint-Pierre before Mont Pelée destroyed it in 1902.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Patrick Chamoiseau, first published 1992. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Texaco, Patrick Chamoiseau (1992). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The rust and ochre of the tin and timber of the hillside shantytown, the creole vitality built from whatever the city discards. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
